cards.back: Unrecoverable past cost shouldn't drive decisions — weigh only future value.
Low skill → overconfidence; the competent are often more cautious.
We favor evidence fitting our beliefs and ignore the rest.
The first number seen skews later judgments.
Losing hurts ~twice as much as gaining feels good.
We judge likelihood by how easily examples come to mind, not real odds.
We overestimate how much others notice us.
We blame others' character but our own slips on circumstances.
We overvalue immediate rewards over bigger later ones.
Fixed: ability is innate. Growth: ability grows with effort.
Unfinished tasks nag at memory; writing them down quiets the loop.
Wording sways choices: "90% lean" feels better than "10% fat".
We copy what others do, especially when unsure — "best seller" works for this.
One good trait (e.g. attractiveness) makes us assume other unrelated virtues.
Bad events hit harder and stick longer than good ones of equal size.
We underestimate how long tasks take; pad estimates and use past data.
Deep absorption when challenge roughly matches skill — too easy bores, too hard stresses.
Cue → routine → reward. Change a habit by keeping the cue/reward, swapping the routine.
We feel obliged to return favors — why free samples drive sales.
Holding conflicting beliefs feels bad, so we rationalize to reduce the tension.
Studying only winners hides the failures, skewing conclusions.
Adding a worse-priced option nudges you toward the target one.
Mondays, birthdays, New Year boost motivation to start new habits — use them.
